edges.description: HMBS deficiency is the enzyme activity abnormality underlying acute intermittent porphyria.
The partial HMBS block creates attack susceptibility when hepatic ALAS1 is induced by physiologic or environmental stressors.
Increased ALAS1-driven upstream flux leads to overproduction of ALA and PBG across the partial HMBS block.
Hepatic overproduction and systemic spillover of ALA produces increased urinary ALA during acute attacks.
Excess hepatic ALA production spills into urine during acute attacks.
Hepatic overproduction and systemic spillover of PBG produces increased urinary PBG during acute attacks.
Excess hepatic PBG production spills into urine during acute attacks.
Excess upstream heme intermediates produce abnormal urinary porphyrin/precursor excretion.
intermediate_mechanisms: urinary excretion of excess heme pathway intermediates

edges.description: Hepatic overproduction and systemic spillover of ALA produces increased plasma ALA during acute attacks.
Hepatic overproduction and systemic spillover of PBG produces increased plasma PBG during acute attacks.
Excess precursor burden is linked to acute autonomic, peripheral, and central neurologic dysfunction.
Abdominal pain is the most common clinical expression of acute neurovisceral crises.
intermediate_mechanisms: autonomic and visceral pain pathways during acute neurovisceral crises

edges.description: Gastrointestinal neurovisceral attacks commonly include nausea and vomiting alongside abdominal pain.
intermediate_mechanisms: gastrointestinal involvement during acute AIP episodes

edges.description: Autonomic gastrointestinal involvement can manifest as constipation.
intermediate_mechanisms: autonomic gastrointestinal dysmotility during neurovisceral attacks

edges.description: Autonomic overactivity during attacks produces tachycardia and other dysautonomic manifestations.
Acute hepatic porphyria attacks can progress to motor-predominant porphyric neuropathy.
intermediate_mechanisms: acute to subacute motor-predominant axonal porphyric neuropathy

edges.description: Acute porphyric neuropathy can involve cranial nerves.
Motor-predominant porphyric neuropathy can cause quadriparesis and respiratory weakness in severe attacks.
intermediate_mechanisms: motor-predominant porphyric neuropathy causing quadriparesis or respiratory weakness

edges.description: Acute AIP presentations can include SIADH-associated hyponatremia as part of the neurovisceral attack spectrum.
intermediate_mechanisms: autonomic and hypothalamic-pituitary dysregulation with inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion

edges.description: Autonomic overactivity during attacks can produce hypertension alongside tachycardia.
Severe porphyric neuropathy can progress to quadriparesis/tetraparesis.
Acute porphyria encephalopathy can include seizures during neuropsychiatric crises.
Acute porphyria encephalopathy can include psychosis during neuropsychiatric crises.
Acute neurovisceral attacks can include central neuropsychiatric deterioration.
Neurovisceral pain during attacks can include back pain.
Neurovisceral and neuropathic pain during attacks can include limb pain.
Neurovisceral pain during attacks can include neck pain.
Increased urinary ALA reports hepatic overproduction and systemic spillover of upstream heme precursors during an active attack.
Lower urinary ALA after ALAS1-suppressive therapy reports reduced hepatic precursor production.
Higher ALA during symptoms supports an ongoing acute attack and can be tracked during treatment response.
Increased urinary PBG reports hepatic overproduction and systemic spillover of upstream heme precursors during an active attack.
Lower urinary PBG after ALAS1-suppressive therapy reports reduced hepatic precursor production.
Higher PBG during symptoms supports an ongoing acute attack and can be tracked during treatment response.
Increased plasma ALA reflects hepatic precursor accumulation during an acute attack.
Increased plasma PBG reflects hepatic precursor accumulation during an acute attack.
Plasma PBG can track the clinical and therapeutic course of acute attacks.

nodes.description: Severe abdominal pain is the most common presenting symptom during acute attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria--impact of mutations found in the hydroxymethylbilane synthase gene on biochemical and enzymatic protein properties.
evidence.snippet: Clinical features include autonomous, central, motor or sensory symptoms, but the most common clinical presentation is abdominal pain caused by neurovisceral crises.

nodes.description: Reduced HMBS/PBGD activity is the enzymatic abnormality underlying AIP.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0012379 | Abnormal enzyme/coenzyme activity | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Back pain is a frequent pain manifestation during acute intermittent porphyria attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0003418 | Back pain | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Constipation is a frequent gastrointestinal manifestation of acute neurovisceral attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002019 | Constipation | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Cranial nerve paralysis is a frequent neurologic manifestation in Orphanet's AIP phenotype table.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0006824 | Cranial nerve paralysis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Urinary ALA is elevated during acute attacks because excess hepatic precursor production spills into urine.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0003163 | Elevated urinary delta-aminolevulinic acid | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Liver-directed siRNA prophylaxis that lowers ALAS1, reduces ALA and PBG, and markedly decreases recurrent attack burden.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and safety of givosiran for acute hepatic porphyria: Final results of the randomized phase III ENVISION trial.
evidence.snippet: These final 36-month results of ENVISION, a phase III study of givosiran in patients with AHP and recurrent attacks, show that long-term monthly treatment with givosiran leads to continuous and sustained reductions in annualized attack rate and use of hemin over time, as well as improved quality of life, with an acceptable safety profile.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria--impact of mutations found in the hydroxymethylbilane synthase gene on biochemical and enzymatic protein properties.
evidence.snippet: Molecular analyses of the hydroxymethylbilane synthase gene revealed seven mutations.

evidence.reference_title: HMBS / acute intermittent porphyria (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: HMBS | HGNC:4982 | acute intermittent porphyria | MONDO:0008294 | SD | Definitive

nodes.description: Pathogenic HMBS variants reduce hepatic hydroxymethylbilane synthase activity, creating a partial block at the third enzymatic step of heme biosynthesis.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria--impact of mutations found in the hydroxymethylbilane synthase gene on biochemical and enzymatic protein properties.
evidence.snippet: Acute intermittent porphyria is an autosomal dominantly inherited disorder, classified as acute hepatic porphyria, caused by a deficiency of hydroxymethylbilane synthase (EC 2.5.1.61, EC 4.3.1.8, also known as porphobilinogen deaminase, uroporphyrinogen I synthase), the third enzyme in heme biosynthesis.

nodes.description: Increased upstream pathway flux in the setting of partial HMBS deficiency causes excess production and systemic spillover of 5-aminolevulinic acid and porphobilinogen.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: As a result of these enzymatic disturbances, heme precursors are synthesized in excess in the liver, and massive amounts of compounds upstream of the enzymatic block are excreted in urine.

evidence.reference_title: RNAi therapy with givosiran significantly reduces attack rates in acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: The clinical manifestations of AHP are attributed to the accumulation of the heme precursor 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and porphobilinogen (PBG).

nodes.description: Acute attacks can feature autonomic overactivity with hypertension alongside tachycardia.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria: response of tachycardia and hypertension to propranolol.
evidence.snippet: In four young adult patients with acute attacks of acute intermittent porphyria tachycardia and hypertension were prominent features of the illness.

nodes.description: Acute attacks can be complicated by hyponatremia, often in the setting of syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion.

evidence.reference_title: Abdominal pain and syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion as clinical presentation of acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Acute intermittent porphyria (AIP) is a rare condition characterized by abdominal pain and a wide range of nonspecific symptoms. We report the case of a woman with abdominal pain and syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) as clinical presentation of AIP.

nodes.description: Urinary PBG is elevated during acute attacks because excess hepatic precursor production spills into urine.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0012217 | Increased urinary porphobilinogen | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Standard treatment for severe acute attacks; exogenous hemin represses hepatic ALAS1 and is more effective than glucose alone in serious attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Plasma porphobilinogen as a sensitive biomarker to monitor the clinical and therapeutic course of acute intermittent porphyria attacks.
evidence.snippet: Treatment is based on symptomatic relief together with carbohydrate loading and in more severe attacks heme therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Porphyric neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: Timely treatment with intravenous heme, carbohydrate loading, and avoidance of porphyrinogenic medications can prevent further neurological morbidity and mortality.

nodes.description: Limb pain is a frequent pain manifestation in Orphanet's AIP phenotype table.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0009763 | Limb pain | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Liver transplantation is a curative option for selected patients with repeated life-threatening acute porphyria attacks or poor quality of life when givosiran is unavailable or insufficiently effective.

evidence.reference_title: Acute Intermittent Porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Liver transplantation, as reported from several centers, is curative. Indications include repeated life-threatening acute porphyria attacks and poor quality of life when givosiran is not available or has shown insufficient medical efficacy.

evidence.reference_title: An update of clinical management of acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: management of patients with recurrent attacks: 1) evaluation of the lifestyle, 2) evaluation of hormonal therapy in women, 3) prophylactic heme therapy, and 4) liver transplantation in patients with severe recurrent attacks.

nodes.description: Mental deterioration is a frequent central neuropsychiatric manifestation in Orphanet's AIP phenotype table.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001268 | Mental deterioration | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Motor-predominant porphyric neuropathy can progress to marked generalized weakness during severe attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Porphyric neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: Acute hepatic porphyrias are inherited metabolic disorders that may present with polyneuropathy, which if not diagnosed early can lead to quadriparesis, respiratory weakness, and death.

nodes.description: Gastrointestinal symptoms during acute attacks commonly extend beyond pain to include nausea and vomiting.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria: focus on possible mechanisms of acute and chronic manifestations.
evidence.snippet: Acute AIP episodes may present with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting, and repeated episodes may result in a series of chronic injuries.

nodes.description: Neck pain is a frequent pain manifestation in Orphanet's AIP phenotype table.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0030833 | Neck pain | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Acute attacks manifest as abdominal pain and neurologic dysfunction, including motor-predominant neuropathy and severe weakness. Accumulated 5-aminolevulinic acid is a recognized pro-oxidant neurotoxin implicated in the neurovisceral manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria--impact of mutations found in the hydroxymethylbilane synthase gene on biochemical and enzymatic protein properties.
evidence.snippet: Clinical features include autonomous, central, motor or sensory symptoms, but the most common clinical presentation is abdominal pain caused by neurovisceral crises.

evidence.reference_title: Porphyric neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: Acute hepatic porphyrias are inherited metabolic disorders that may present with polyneuropathy, which if not diagnosed early can lead to quadriparesis, respiratory weakness, and death.

nodes.description: Ovulation suppression is an alternative prophylactic strategy for women with recurrent attacks when givosiran is not available, addressing hormonally triggered attack recurrence.

evidence.reference_title: Acute Intermittent Porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Alternative medical therapies to reduce frequency and/or severity of acute attacks when givosiran is not available include suppression of ovulation and prophylactic hemin infusion.

evidence.reference_title: An update of clinical management of acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: management of patients with recurrent attacks: 1) evaluation of the lifestyle, 2) evaluation of hormonal therapy in women, 3) prophylactic heme therapy, and 4) liver transplantation in patients with severe recurrent attacks.

nodes.description: Acute attacks can include a predominantly motor polyneuropathy that may progress to quadriparesis and respiratory weakness if not recognized early.

evidence.reference_title: Porphyric neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: Acute hepatic porphyrias are inherited metabolic disorders that may present with polyneuropathy, which if not diagnosed early can lead to quadriparesis, respiratory weakness, and death.

evidence.reference_title: Plasma porphobilinogen as a sensitive biomarker to monitor the clinical and therapeutic course of acute intermittent porphyria attacks.
evidence.snippet: During an acute attack the heme precursors porphobilinogen (PBG) and 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) are produced in high amounts by the liver and are found in high concentrations in plasma and urine.

readouts.interpretation: Increased plasma ALA reflects hepatic precursor accumulation during an acute attack.

evidence.reference_title: Plasma porphobilinogen as a sensitive biomarker to monitor the clinical and therapeutic course of acute intermittent porphyria attacks.
evidence.snippet: During an acute attack the heme precursors porphobilinogen (PBG) and 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) are produced in high amounts by the liver and are found in high concentrations in plasma and urine.

readouts.interpretation: Increased plasma PBG reflects hepatic precursor accumulation during an acute attack.
Plasma PBG can track the clinical and therapeutic course of acute attacks.

nodes.description: Porphyrinuria reflects increased urinary excretion of heme pathway intermediates during active disease.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0010473 | Porphyrinuria | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Scheduled hemin infusion is an alternative prophylactic therapy to reduce frequency or severity of recurrent acute attacks when givosiran is not available.

evidence.reference_title: Acute Intermittent Porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Alternative medical therapies to reduce frequency and/or severity of acute attacks when givosiran is not available include suppression of ovulation and prophylactic hemin infusion.

evidence.reference_title: An update of clinical management of acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: management of patients with recurrent attacks: 1) evaluation of the lifestyle, 2) evaluation of hormonal therapy in women, 3) prophylactic heme therapy, and 4) liver transplantation in patients with severe recurrent attacks.

nodes.description: Acute neuropsychiatric crises can include encephalopathy with psychosis.

evidence.reference_title: An update of clinical management of acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Intensive abdominal pain without peritoneal signs, acute peripheral neuropathy, and encephalopathy usually with seizures or psychosis are the key symptoms indicating possible acute porphyria.

nodes.description: Acute neuropsychiatric crises can include encephalopathy with seizures.

evidence.reference_title: An update of clinical management of acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Intensive abdominal pain without peritoneal signs, acute peripheral neuropathy, and encephalopathy usually with seizures or psychosis are the key symptoms indicating possible acute porphyria.

nodes.description: Acute neurovisceral attacks often feature autonomic overactivity with sinus tachycardia and accompanying hypertension.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria: response of tachycardia and hypertension to propranolol.
evidence.snippet: In four young adult patients with acute attacks of acute intermittent porphyria tachycardia and hypertension were prominent features of the illness.

nodes.description: Severe motor-predominant porphyric neuropathy can progress to quadriparesis or tetraparesis.

evidence.reference_title: Porphyric neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: Acute hepatic porphyrias are inherited metabolic disorders that may present with polyneuropathy, which if not diagnosed early can lead to quadriparesis, respiratory weakness, and death.

nodes.description: Porphyrogenic triggers increase hepatic ALAS1 expression, pushing pathway flux into the segment upstream of the HMBS block.

evidence.reference_title: Acute intermittent porphyria.
evidence.snippet: Biochemical abnormalities are thought to result from primary defects of porphobilinogen deaminase (PBGD; also called hydroxymethyl bilane synthase), the third enzyme of the heme synthesis pathway, and consecutive hepatic overexpression of the first enzyme of the pathway, 5-aminolevulinate synthase.

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent attacks of acute hepatic porphyria: major role of the chronic inflammatory response in the liver.
evidence.snippet: Acute intermittent porphyria (AIP) is an inherited disorder of haem metabolism characterized by life-threatening acute neurovisceral attacks due to the induction of hepatic δ-aminolevulinic acid synthase 1 (ALAS1) associated with hydroxymethylbilane synthase (HMBS) deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Plasma porphobilinogen as a sensitive biomarker to monitor the clinical and therapeutic course of acute intermittent porphyria attacks.
evidence.snippet: During an acute attack the heme precursors porphobilinogen (PBG) and 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) are produced in high amounts by the liver and are found in high concentrations in plasma and urine.

readouts.interpretation: Increased urinary ALA reports hepatic overproduction and systemic spillover of upstream heme precursors during an active attack.
Lower urinary ALA after ALAS1-suppressive therapy reports reduced hepatic precursor production.
Higher ALA during symptoms supports an ongoing acute attack and can be tracked during treatment response.

evidence.reference_title: Plasma porphobilinogen as a sensitive biomarker to monitor the clinical and therapeutic course of acute intermittent porphyria attacks.
evidence.snippet: During an acute attack the heme precursors porphobilinogen (PBG) and 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) are produced in high amounts by the liver and are found in high concentrations in plasma and urine.

readouts.interpretation: Increased urinary PBG reports hepatic overproduction and systemic spillover of upstream heme precursors during an active attack.
Lower urinary PBG after ALAS1-suppressive therapy reports reduced hepatic precursor production.
Higher PBG during symptoms supports an ongoing acute attack and can be tracked during treatment response.
